Summary of the hardness values for various individual phases and that of the combinations of phases. sample annealed at $5 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and $6 . 7 G P a$ for α+ needle-shape κ in the sample annealed at $7 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . κ-particles precipitated at $7 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ contained higher Mn (≈24.8 wt.%) compared to those formed at $5 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ( \approx 1 8 . 4 w t . \% )$ meaning that a higher annealing temperature afects the formation energy, lattice parameter and magnetization of κ-phase resulting in the formation of harder κ particles. Tis was confrmed by our ab-initio calculations which are discussed later in this paper. At higher temperatures, when the precipitates changed to FeAl-type B2, the hardness increased to $8 . 9 \dot { G } P a$ for $\alpha + \mathrm { B } 2$ and 8.3 GPa in the samples annealed at $9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and $1 0 \dot { 5 } 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ Al content of the particles ranged from 22.8 to 24.6 wt.%.

3. For the Ni-containing samples, the precipitates changed for all annealing temperatures to NiAl-type B2 and the measured hardness values were for the composite structures: 11.7 GPa for $\alpha + \mathrm { B } 2$ for samples annealed at ${ 9 0 0 ^ { \circ } } \mathrm { C } ,$ and13.5 GPa for $\alpha + \mathrm { B } 2$ for samples annealed at $1 0 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ At lower temperatures, the B2 precipitates changed to stringer bands and resulted in hardness values of 10.1 GPa for samples annealed at $5 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and $1 0 . 6 G \dot { P } a$ for stringer bands annealed at $7 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . It was found that the variation of hardness values of B2 in Ni-containing sample is a function of Ni content. Increasing annealing temperature resulted in an increase in the Ni content of B2 particles which ultimately led to an increment in the hardness values. To summarize the results for the hardness of the γ phase and the composites formed with the γ phase:
4. Te hardness was found to be approximately 4.2 GPa and 4.0 GPa for the solution-treated single γ-phase in both Ni and Ni-free samples respectively.

5. For the Ni-containing samples the hardness was measured to be 6.7–7.5 GPa for γ + cuboid κ and 8.8 $- 9 G P a$ for the γ + cuboid $\kappa + \mathtt { B } 2$ composite phase for samples annealed at $5 0 0 { - } 7 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ respecively. At higher annealing temperatures $9 0 0 { - } 1 \dot { 0 } \dot { 5 } 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ only B2 precipitated and the measured hardness values were $7 . { \overset { \cdot } { 9 } } - 8 . 2 G P a \ \gamma + \mathbf { B } 2$ for the sample annealed at 900 and $1 0 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ respecitively.

(a) Te load-depth curves of NiAl-type nanosized disk-like B2 phase formed within an α matrix in the Ni-containing sample for the four annealing treatments and (b) the corresponding hardness values. (c) Te load-depth curves of coarse B2 particles precipitated in the γ matrix in the Ni-containing sample and (d) their corresponding hardness values. (e) Te load-depth curves of NiAl-type nanosized disk-like B2 phase formed within an α matrix in the Ni-free sample for the four annealing treatments and (f) the corresponding hardness values. (g) Te load-depth curves of coarse B2 particles precipitated in the γ matrix in the Ni-free sample and (h) their corresponding hardness values. 700 °C. At higher annealing temperatures B2 was the only phase to precipitate but the hardness did not change signifcantly (7.2 GPa for γ + B2 in the sample annealed at 900 °C and 7 GPa for the γ + B2 in the same sample at 1050 °C. Te trends listed in 1–6 suggests that the hardness increases in the composite structures resulting from higher temperature annealing, when precipitates are predominantly B2 and the highest hardness is found in the structure disk-like B2 formed in the ferrite (hardness value = 10 GPa). According to Orowan mechanism, the increase in shear stress (Δτ) is proportional to the inverse of the precipitates’ diameter: $\Delta \tau \propto \mathrm { R } ^ { - 1 2 5 }$ . In other words, as the size of precipitates decreases the resistance to shear increases. Tus, the higher hardness values obtained at higher annealing temperatures can be also attributed to the formation of uniformly distributed nano-sized disk-like B2 particles which increase the shear resistance of the α-matrix10. It can be seen that the hardness of γ + (nanosized-κ-carbide) + (microsized-B2) composite reached a higher value than γ + micron sized B2 composite. Tis is despite the fact that the κ-carbides are known to not be as resistant to shear as B2 intermetallic compounds. Terefore, the addition of brittle nano-sized κ-carbide to γ + micron sized B2 composite increase the hardness further. Te hardness values of the coarse NiAl-type B2 particles precipitated in the γ phase of Ni-containing sample was less than those of the corresponding precipitates in the α (Fig. 5d). Tis phenomenon occurs mainly due to the Ni atoms primarily accumulated in the α phase to form the B2 stringer bands at the two lower annealing temperatures. At higher annealing temperatures, more Ni atoms were available to form the nanosized-B2 particles in the α phase. Terefore, hardness values increase from 9.1 GPa (Ni: 8.2 wt.%) at $5 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ to 10.5 GPa (Ni: 10.1 wt.%) at 1050 °C. However, for Ni-free sample, the values of coarse FeAl-type B2 particles in the γ and nano-sized B2 particles did not difer signifcantly (Fig. 5h). Te values were recorded to be 9.2 GPa for annealing at 900 °C (8.9 GPa for nanosized B2 in α formed during the same treatment) and 9 GPa for annealing at $1 0 5 0 ^ { \circ } \bar { \mathrm { C } }$ (8.3 GPa for the same phase in α precipitated during the same treatment). Te Al content of B2 particles formed in γ phase was less than those formed in α (Al is an α stabiliser) and this was anticipated to lead to an increase in the hardness of the particles. For a detailed discussion on the efects of size and morphology of ordered precipitates on the mechanical properties of low-density steels, the reader is referred to refs10,11. Te nanoindentation technique as performed in this study was only able to measure the hardness of the nano-sized features as present within the surrounding matrix, and therefore yields a compound hardness value of the matrix with particles. To evaluate the possible size-efect of the diferently sized phases on the hardness of the matrix and particle composite, it is required to perform nanoindentation using diferent indentors or of diferent applied loads. Unfortunately, changing the indentation size or volume will further introduce a nano-indentation size-efect as a function of varying size and shape of the plastic zone below the indent26,27 which may obscure the real infuences of the various precipitates and their sizes on the hardness of the composite microstructure. DFT calculations. We performed frst-principle calculations to determine the lattice parameter and thus elastic constants of κ-carbide. Although, the calculated lattice parameter of the ordered precipitate FeAl-B2 for cases with or without spin efects (spin-polarized (sp), without spin efects (np), the blue diamond in Fig. 6) was slightly less than the experimental value (red triangle), the values were still in good agreement with the reported experimental ones. Te calculations for without spin efects were performed as it was reported in the literature that the perfect FeAl-B2 phase does not exhibit a macroscopic magnetic momentum28. Calculations were also carried out for the $\mathrm { F e } _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l - L } 1 _ { 2 }$ compound for comparison with the Fe AlC phase. Te lattice constant for $\mathrm { F e } _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l C }$ was calculated to be larger than that for Fe Al-L1 which agrees well with the experimental observations reported 3 2in refs29,30. Te insertion of the C atom in the crystal structure of $\mathrm { L } 1 _ { 2 }$ in the octahedral interstitial site induces an expansion of the lattice parameter. Te calculations showed that the substitution of the Fe atoms by the Mn atoms increases the lattice parameters of the structure from 3.78 A for Fe MnAlC to 3.84 A for the Mn AlC phase29,31,32. Tis increase in the lattice parameter because of insertion of additional Mn atoms was believed to be due to the larger atomic radii of Mn $( r _ { M n } = 1 . 6 1 \dot { A } )$ compared to that of Fe $( r _ { F e } = 1 . 5 6 \dot { A } ) ^ { 3 3 }$ 3. ![](images/4e3c8d2239814a5e27d0827467957549d9becc0d117e17bd2fdae7bd27df295d.jpg) <details> <summary>scatter</summary> | Material | Lattice parameter α₀ (Å) | | :--- | :--- | | FeAl - B2 | 2.9 | | Fe₃Al - L₁₂ | 3.6 | | Fe₃AlC - L1'₂ | 3.75 | | Fe₂MnAlC | 3.78 | | FeMn₂AlC | 3.78 | | Mn₃AlC | 3.85 | | exp | 3.85 | </details> Figure 6. Te lattice constants for diferent ordered compounds: FeAl-B2, $F e _ { 3 } A l { - } L 1 _ { 2 } , F e _ { 3 } A l C { - } L 1 _ { 2 } , F e _ { 2 } M n A l C ,$ , $F e \bar { M } n _ { 2 } A l C ,$ , and $M n _ { 3 } A l C .$ Experimental values are indicated by red triangles. DFT calculation $\mathbf { i } ^ { 3 4 , 3 5 }$ of the efects of increasing Al on the elastic constants of Fe-Al compounds showed that as the composition changes from FeAl to FeAl2 and FeAl3 the $\mathrm { C } _ { 1 1 }$ elastic constant decreases from 294 to 223 and 168.5 (all in GPa) respectively, while the value of $\mathrm { C } _ { 4 4 }$ reduced from 157 to 109.6 and 76.1 GPa when the structure changed from FeAl to FeAl and FeAl respectively. On the other hand, for Ni-Al compounds, ab-initio calculations36 showed that $\mathrm { C } _ { 1 1 }$ increased from 221.67 to 375.61 GPa and $\mathrm { C } _ { 4 4 }$ increased from 116.99 to 133.21 GPa by changing the structure from NiAl to $\mathrm { N i } _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l } .$ . From values of elastic constants of the two compounds, Ni-Al compounds have elastic constants with higher values compared to Fe-Al compounds. In addition to that Breuer et al. 37 conducted a series of experiments and measured the enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm { { B } 2 \mathrm { - F e _ { 1 \mathrm { - x } } \mathrm { - A l } } }$ and $\mathrm { B } 2 \mathrm { - } \mathrm { ( F e , N i ) _ { 1 - x } \mathrm { - } A l } .$ at 1073 K. Teir results showed that generally, enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm { B } 2 \mathrm { - } \left( \mathrm { F e } , \mathrm { N i } \right) _ { \mathrm { 1 - x } } \mathrm { - A l _ { x } }$ is of greater magnitude than that of B2- $\cdot \mathrm { F e } _ { \mathrm { 1 - x } } \mathrm { - A l } _ { \mathrm { x } } .$ It was also shown that starting with binary B2- $\mathrm { F e } _ { \mathrm { 1 - x } } { \mathrm { - A l } } _ { \mathrm { x } }$ and replacing Fe with Ni, $\mathrm { B } 2 \mathrm { - } \mathrm { ( F e , N i ) _ { 1 - x } \mathrm { - } A l } .$ x while keeping the Al content at a constant value, the enthalpy of formation becomes increasingly more negative37. In other words, increasing the Ni content of B2 can result in the formation of a harder B2 phase. Tis is supported by the experimental results in Table 2 when comparing the composite structures, with Ni vs. without Ni, containing B2 in both austenite and ferrite. Higher annealing temperatures led to an increase in the Ni content of B2 phase. As is obvious from Table 2, the hardness values increased for those B2 particles formed at higher temperatures. Te ferromagnetic and non-magnetic states of ordered Fe3AlC were also investigated. Te ground state was calculated to be 30meV/at in favour of the ferromagnetic state with a magnetic moment $( 1 . 1 \mu _ { B } / a t . F e )$ more than twice smaller than in Fe Al. To study why the magnetic moment decreased compared to the $\mathrm { L } 1 _ { 2 }$ compound, we investigated the role of the lattice parameter on the magnetic moment. A numerical study was performed in which the lattice parameter of $\mathrm { F e } _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l C }$ was imposed to $\mathrm { F e } _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l } .$ . Te results showed that the decrease of the magnetism from $\operatorname { L } 1 _ { 2 }$ to imposed-L12 was not due to the increase in the lattice parameter $( \mu ^ { L 1 _ { 2 } } ( a _ { 0 } = 3 . 7 6 ) \overline { { = } }$ $2 . 3 9 > \mu ^ { L 1 _ { 2 } } ( a _ { 0 } = 3 . 6 2 ) = 2 . 2 4 )$ . It was therefore assumed that the decrease in the magnetism is due to the interactions between Fe and C atoms. Te total Density of States (DOS) of the four crystalline  κ-carbides are presented in Fig. 7. Te overall shapes of the four structures were very similar. Te only diference was the so-called ‘rigid band shif’: the shif of the peaks to higher energy or equivalently the shif of the Fermi level to lower energy. Tis was believed to result from two efects: frst Mn has one less electron than Fe, and second, the replacement of Fe by Mn barely produces a change in the electronic states around the Fermi level. Te vibrational band structures for FeAl, Fe Al and Fe AlC phases were calculated with the SGGA approximation. In the case of FeAl (Fig. 8a), the high-frequency branches were initiated mainly from the low mass Al atoms whereas the low-frequency bands primarily originated from the high-mass Fe atoms. Tese two bands were separated by an optical gap. Te optical gap (around 0.7 THz) had the same width as that calculated for FeAl-B2 or $\mathrm { D O } 3 ^ { \dot { 3 } 8 }$ , while the results on $\mathbf { \bar { F } e } _ { 3 } \mathbf { A l - } \mathbf { \bar { L } } \bar { 1 } _ { 2 }$ were close to those calculated for $\mathrm { N i } _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l - L } 1 _ { 2 } { } ^ { 3 9 }$ . As for $\mathrm { F e } _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l }$ (Fig. 8b), the result showed that the structure is stable, although it was reported in the literature that within the GGA approximation the L1 structure is not mechanically stable38. Tis anomaly between the present calculation and those reported in the literature showed that the properties are very sensitive to the choice of the functional. Te vibrational band structure for $\mathrm { F e } _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l C }$ was shown in Fig. 8c. In contrast to the previous calculation for $\mathrm { F e } _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l }$ , no signifcant diferences were noted between the present calculations (GGA approach) and those reported in the literature (SGGA approach). ![](images/9c697cad63508ed0ee3857f7e47c2b678cd88f3eef8da0a71a7d03124e9b2617.jpg) Figure 7. Total density of states of (a) Fe3AlC, (b) Fe2MnAlC, (c) ${ \mathrm { F e M n } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { A l C } } ,$ and (d) Mn3AlC. We used two methods to evaluate the elastic constants of diferent compounds under study: Te elastic constants $C _ { i j }$ were derived from the vibrational acoustic dispersion curves presented above. For the cubic systems, only three elastic constants, namely $C _ { 1 1 } , C _ { 1 2 } ,$ and $C _ { 4 4 } ,$ are independent. •	 In the second method, a couple of lattice distortions was used to determine the elastic constants40–42. Te detailed descriptions of both methods are explained in the methods section. Te calculation results are summarized in Table 3. For $\mathrm { F e } _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l }$ the elastic constants were similar to other Fe-Al ordered phases. Te occupation fo the octahedral interstitial site by C atoms rigidifed the $\mathrm { F e } _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l C }$ in comparison with $F e _ { 3 } A l .$ . Tis is the direct manifestation of the strong Fe-C bonds in the $<$ 100 directions. Te calculations revealed that all elastic constants of ${ \mathrm { M n } } _ { 3 } { \mathrm { A l C } }$ are greater than those of $\mathrm { F e } _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l C }$ confrming the experimental hardness values reported above (the increase in the Mn content of κ-carbide resulted in an increase in the hardness value of the phase). Tis is also a direct consequence of strong Mn-C bonds which are harder than Fe-C bonds. For the tetragonal structures, Fe MnAlC and FeMn AlC, $C _ { 1 1 }$ and $C _ { 3 3 }$ are predominantly greater than the other elastic constants indicating the formation of strong Mn-C bonds in 〈100〉 directions. From the calculated set of elastic constants, practical parameters such as bulk modulus $B _ { 0 } ,$ the factor of anisotropy $C _ { a } ,$ , the shear modulus $G ,$ and the Young’s modulus E in two crystallographic directions ([100] and [111]) were calculated (see methods) and listed in Table 4. Te Fe AlC phase showed a large bulk modulus and also a strong degree of anisotropy $( C _ { a } = 2 . 3 9$ from the frst method and 2.47 from the second method). Tis anisotropy was also showed by phase-feld modelling of κ-carbide9 and also confrmed by experiments11. It is expected in metals, that $C _ { a }$ is smaller than 1 but these calculations showed that this value for κ was greater than 1. Tis led $E _ { 1 1 1 }$ to be smaller than $E _ { 1 0 0 } .$ . In addition, the degree of anisotropy for FeAl compound was small $( C _ { a } = 0 . 5 4 )$ from the frst method and 0.53 from the second method). Tis low degree of anisotropy was also in good agreement with the phase-feld simulation of the B2 intermetallic compound11. Again, upon the substitution of Fe atoms by Mn ones, the various properties such as bulk modulus, shear modulus, and Young’s modulus increased confrming the experimentally observed increase in the hardness of the κ phase enriched by Mn atoms. Tis is supported by the experimental results (point 2 in the results section), wherein the κ-ferrite hardness was higher in the structures with higher Mn containing precipitates obtained at 700 °C compared to those obtained at $\mathsf { \Pi } _ { 5 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } } ^ { }$ . In addition, the shear modulus of $\mathrm { F e } _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l } \check { \mathrm { C } }$ was greater than that of FeAl or $\mathrm { F e } _ { 3 } \mathrm { \bar { A l } }$ confrming the un-shearable nature of these precipitates that was observed experimentally. # Conclusions Afer annealing at temperatures between 500 and 1050 °C the hardness of micro- and nano-structural features found in Fe-15Mn-10Al-0.8C-5Ni and Fe-15Mn-10Al-0.8 C low-density steels were investigated experimentally through nano-indentation and computationally through ab-initio calculations. Te hardness of micro-structures with B2 precipitates was measured through nano-indentation, whereas the hardness of nano-sized κ phase was evaluated through ab-initio calculations. Te addition of Ni increased the hardness of the B2 phase due to the replacement of Ni-Al bonds over the Fe-Al bonds. In the presence of Ni in the alloy, the peak hardness increased from 7.2 GPa to 9 GPa. Tis was observed in both the austenite and the ferrite phases. ![](images/153fcef7243603d94b57102841fa77209d9074bd55d99797ff9210f63f5f6391.jpg) <details> <summary>line</summary> | Point | Energy (THz) | |-------|--------------| | Γ     | 0            | | M     | 8            | | R     | 6            | | Γ'    | 0            | | X     | 7            | </details> ![](images/8324447b8c8c09e223377719123d98d8542d23e562562c9d50df4e4902ae3d1f.jpg) <details> <summary>line</summary> | Point | Energy (THz) | |-------|--------------| | Γ     | 0            | | X     | ~6           | | M     | ~8           | | R     | ~10          | | Γ     | 0            | </details> ![](images/d9856aed57c309699cdcc926e455c8b4f045937c9155da69558df6877490b0b5.jpg) <details> <summary>line</summary> | Point | Energy (THz) | |-------|--------------| | Γ     | 0            | | X     | 25           | | M     | 20           | | R     | 15           | | Γ'    | 0            | </details> Figure 8. Te values in parenthesis were calculated using the second method (lattice distortion). <table><tr><td>Compound</td><td colspan="2"> $B_0$ </td><td colspan="2"> $C_\alpha$ </td><td colspan="2">G</td><td colspan="2"> $E_{100}$ </td><td colspan="2"> $E_{111}$ </td></tr><tr><td>FeAl</td><td>164</td><td>-179</td><td>0.54</td><td>-0.53</td><td>81.5</td><td>-85.5</td><td>210</td><td>-221</td><td>333</td><td>-370</td></tr><tr><td> $Fe_3Al$ </td><td>153</td><td>-171</td><td>0.12</td><td>0.08</td><td>21</td><td>-9.5</td><td>60</td><td>-28</td><td>322</td><td>-270</td></tr><tr><td> $Fe_3AlC(sp)$ </td><td>216</td><td>-206</td><td>2.39</td><td>-2.47</td><td>175</td><td>-168.5</td><td>477</td><td>-397</td><td>217</td><td>-227</td></tr><tr><td> $Fe_2AlC$ </td><td>—</td><td>-195</td><td>—</td><td>-1.8</td><td>—</td><td>-173.5</td><td>—</td><td>-402</td><td>—</td><td>-256</td></tr><tr><td>FeMnAlC</td><td>—</td><td>-201</td><td>—</td><td>-1.94</td><td>—</td><td>-195</td><td>—</td><td>-446</td><td>—</td><td>-263</td></tr><tr><td> $Mn_3AlC$ </td><td>—</td><td>-214</td><td>—</td><td>-1.59</td><td>—</td><td>-175</td><td>—</td><td>-412</td><td>—</td><td>-285</td></tr></table> Table 4. Alternative set of elastic parameters: the bulk modulus $B _ { 0 }$ (in GPa), the factor of anisotropy $C _ { a } ,$ the shear modulus G (in GPa), and the Young’s modulus in two crystallographic directions $E _ { 1 0 0 }$ and $E _ { 1 1 1 }$ (in GPa). Te nano-sized cuboid κ and micron-sized B2 phases obtained in austenite when the Ni containing samples were annealed at $7 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ or lower temperature improved the hardness further in the austenite phase. Te γ phase in samples annealed at higher temperatures $\mathsf { \bar { ( 9 0 0 - 1 0 5 0 ^ { \circ } C ) } }$ , resulted in lower hardness due to the instability of nano-sized cuboid κ. Te ferritic phase obtained in the Ni-containing alloys annealed at 900–1050 °C, produced a hard structure of 10 GPa due to the dispersion of nano-sized discs of B2 phase. Based on the frst-principles calculations of diferent ordered structures, it was found that the insertion of a C atom only enhances the elastic constant in the κ-phase $( \mathrm { L } 1 _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } )$ whereas it has a lesser efect on the $\mathrm { F e } _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l } -$ L1 phase. However, the presence of Mn atoms in the structure of Fe AlC−κ increases its elastic constant. Tis was supported by the experimental results. # Methods ab-initio calculations. In the frst method, the slope of the acoustic branches in the [100] direction was used to calculate $C _ { 1 1 }$ and $C _ { 4 4 } \mathrm { : }$ 14号 $$ \nu_ {l} ^ {[ 1 0 0 ]} = \sqrt {\frac {C _ {1 1}}{\rho}} \tag {1} $$ $$ \nu_ {t} ^ {[ 1 0 0 ]} = \sqrt {\frac {C _ {4 4}}{\rho}} \tag {2} $$ where $\nu _ { l } ^ { [ 1 0 0 ] }$ and $\nu _ { t } ^ { [ 1 0 0 ] }$ are the longitudinal and transverse velocity of sound in the compound, respectively. ρ is the density of the compound: for FeAl 5839, for $\mathrm { F e } _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l }$ 6612 and for Fe3AlC 6527 (all in kg/m3 ). [111] direction was used to calculate the value of $C _ { 1 2 } \colon$ $$ \nu_ {l} ^ {[ 1 1 1 ]} = \sqrt {\frac {C _ {1 1} + 2 C _ {1 2} + 4 C _ {4 4}}{3 \rho}} \tag {3} $$ In the second method, a volume conserving orthorhombic distortion $$ \epsilon = \left( \begin{array}{c c c} \delta & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & - \delta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac {\delta^ {2}}{1 - \delta^ {2}} \end{array} \right) \tag {4} $$ the associated change in total energy can be written as $$ E (\delta) = E (0) + \left(C _ {1 1} - C _ {1 2}\right) V \delta^ {2} + O \left(\delta^ {4}\right) \tag {5} $$ where V is the volume of the unit cell. Tis energy was solved to determine $C _ { 1 1 }$ and $C _ { 1 2 } .$ For $C _ { 4 4 } ,$ a monoclinic distortion $$ \epsilon = \left( \begin{array}{c c c} 0 & \frac {\delta}{2} & 0 \\ \frac {\delta}{2} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac {\delta^ {2}}{4 - \delta^ {2}} \end{array} \right) \tag {6} $$ results in a total energy change as following: $$ E (\delta) = E (0) + \frac {1}{2} C _ {4 4} V \delta^ {2} + O (\delta^ {4}) \tag {7} $$ For a tetragonal crystal structure, three additional independent elastic constant exists, namely $C _ { 1 3 } , C _ { 3 3 } ,$ and $C _ { 6 6 } .$ Te scheme presented in the ref.40 was used to determine these elastic constants for $\mathrm { F e } _ { 2 } \mathrm { M n A l C }$ and FeMn2AlC phases. Te practical parameters are calculated according to the following equations40: $$ B _ {0} = \frac {C _ {1 1} + 2 C _ {1 2}}{3} \tag {8} $$ $$ C _ {a} = \frac {C _ {1 1} - C _ {1 2}}{2 C _ {4 4}} \tag {9} $$ $$ G = \frac {C _ {1 1} - C _ {1 2}}{2} \tag {10} $$ $$ E _ {1 0 0} = \frac {\left(C _ {1 1} + 2 C _ {1 2}\right) \left(C _ {1 1} - C _ {1 2}\right)}{C _ {1 1} + C _ {1 2}} \tag {11} $$ $$ E _ {1 1 1} = \left(\frac {1}{E _ {1 0 0}} + \frac {C _ {1 1} - C _ {1 2} - 2 C _ {4 4}}{3 C _ {4 4} (C _ {1 1} - C _ {1 2})}\right) ^ {- 1} \tag {12} $$
